## Persona

A parent in central Iowa whose adult child lives with bipolar disorder. They have lived through escalations where calling 911 felt like a last resort because they couldn't predict how the responding officer would react. They've read the news. They know how a uniform, a raised voice, or a misread movement can turn a crisis into a tragedy. They're searching at 2am after a hard night, trying to learn — before the next bad night — whether there's a way to call for help that won't risk making things worse. They are not naïve about the system. They want plain, honest information about what's actually available in their county and what isn't.

## Research summary

Inside Out already operates a CIT Co-Responder Program in partnership with **Heart of Iowa Community Services (HICS)**. The model pairs an Inside Out mental health professional with a CIT-trained officer who respond together in an unmarked vehicle and plain clothes. The team is **currently active in four Dallas County / west-metro police jurisdictions: Clive, Urbandale, Waukee, and West Des Moines.** (Reference: insideoutiowa.com/services/crisis-intervention-training-program.) HICS reporting cites 367+ jail diversions, 2,486+ calls served, and 370+ officers trained across the Heart of Iowa region (Waukee, Clive, Urbandale, West Des Moines, Dallas County, Adel, Guthrie County, Woodward, Perry) — though Justin should confirm which figures Inside Out is comfortable citing publicly. The strongest public-facing models in the field (Denver STAR, Foundation 2 in Linn County, NAMI Iowa's coverage) succeed by leading with what the response *looks and sounds like* on the ground — "no sirens, no flashing lights, no badge", "go low and slow", introduce themselves by first name, "we came to help, not to arrest or hurt" — rather than program structure. The weakest public communication (Cedar Rapids PD, most county sheriff pages) treats CIT as an org-chart bullet inside a police website and offers nothing to a frightened family member. This draft positions Inside Out's page closer to STAR's voice while keeping the operational facts from Inside Out's existing copy.

## What landed / what didn't

**Inside Out's existing CIT page (insideoutiowa.com)**
- Landed: The 6-step "How CIT navigates a call" walkthrough is honest and concrete. "Unlimited amount of time to spend with the individual. There is no sense of urgency to jump to the next call." — that line alone tells a worried parent more than most program descriptions. The FAQ on plain clothes / unmarked car is gold — it directly addresses the embarrassment-and-stigma concern.
- Didn't: Buries the lede. The persona reads the H1 "Partnering mental health professionals with first responders" and doesn't know yet whether this helps *them*. "Navigates a call" is corporate. "People in need are less likely to be sent to jail" is good but should not be the first reassurance — the first reassurance should be about how the person is *treated*, not where they end up.

**Heart of Iowa Community Services (hicsiowa.org)**
- Landed: Specific numbers (367 diversions, 2,486 calls). Concrete partner list. "98% of cases now served through this model were kept out of jail" — strong, if Justin wants to use it.
- Didn't: Reads like an annual report. Addresses funders and county officials, not the parent on the phone at 2am.

**Cedar Rapids Police CIT page**
- Landed: Nothing. It is an org-chart entry buried in a left-rail of 40+ police-department subpages — Cops 'N Kids, McGruff the Crime Dog, Sex Crimes, K9 Unit. The persona scrolling past "Special Response Team" while looking for a mental-health response is the opposite of what we want.
- Didn't: The framing ("composed of specially trained officers whose function is to respond to incidents") uses every red-flag word — "function", "respond to incidents", "subjects". This is the tone the Inside Out page must explicitly *not* adopt.

**Denver STAR (wellpower.org, denvergov.org)**
- Landed: "It doesn't arrive with sirens or flashing lights. It doesn't wear a badge or carry restraints. It shows up in a van, staffed by a paramedic and a clinician, ready to listen, support and connect." This is the single most trust-building paragraph in any program I reviewed. It directly answers the question "will this feel different from cops showing up?" before the reader has to ask. Also strong: harm-reduction and trauma-informed language stated up front.
- Didn't: STAR is a clinician-only model, not co-responder, so the comparison is imperfect — but the *voice* is the model.

**Foundation 2 / Linn County (foundation2.org, linncountyiowa.gov)**
- Landed: Pairs the co-responder service with a 24/7 Mental Health Access Center as the "what's next" — clearly explains that the responder hands off to ongoing care. Useful structural idea.
- Didn't: Public-facing language still leans clinical and county-administrative.

**NAMI Iowa coverage (namiiowa.org)**
- Landed: "Go low and slow." "Introduce themselves to the person using their first name and then assure them they came to help them, not to arrest or hurt them." These are the exact phrases the persona needs to read. They name the fear without dramatizing it.
- Didn't: It's an advocacy article, not a service page, so it's descriptive rather than action-oriented.

**Polk County / Broadlawns / Mobile Crisis Response Team**
- Landed: Concrete cohort makeup (two nurses, one therapist, one social worker), concrete hours (8am–midnight), concrete outcomes (2,625 calls, 21 arrests). Honest about scope limits.
- Didn't: Distinguishing CIT from MCRT is genuinely confusing across Iowa websites. The Inside Out page must make this distinction cleanly because the persona is going to land here and on the Mobile Crisis page and need to know which one to look at.

## Tone notes

- Name the fear of bad police outcomes in the first 200 words, then explain what CIT changes about that. Do not pretend the fear isn't there.
- Lead with what the response *looks and sounds like on a doorstep*, not with program structure. Pictures first, org chart second.
- Plain clothes and unmarked car are not a detail — they're the headline. Surface them early.
- Acknowledge limits honestly: CIT runs in specific jurisdictions, not statewide. If the caller's town doesn't have CIT yet, the page should tell them where to call instead and not pretend coverage is universal.
- No police-speak: avoid "subject," "incident," "response unit," "deploy," "secure the scene." Officer is fine; "first responder" is fine in some contexts.
- No savior tone. The team is competent, not heroic. The person in crisis is not "rescued."
- Acknowledge that calling 911 is hard. Don't talk people out of calling — talk them through what to say.

## Content outline

### Hero
- **H1:** A different kind of response when mental health and 911 meet
- **Sub:** When someone you love is in crisis, you shouldn't have to choose between getting help and risking how police will react. Our Crisis Intervention Team pairs a mental health professional with a CIT-trained officer so the door that opens is the right one. They arrive in plain clothes, in an unmarked car, with time to actually listen.
- **Primary CTA:** Call 844-428-3878 — Inside Out Crisis Line *(button label: "Get Help Now — Call 844-428-3878")*
- **Secondary CTA:** See how a CIT response works → `#how-it-works`

*Notes for build: The sub is intentionally longer than the Adults Crisis Stabilization sub because CIT requires more demystification — the term is unfamiliar and the reader is frightened. Flagging this for the implementer.*

### Stats row
*(Placeholder stats per Justin's 2026-05-22 directive — confirm or swap for Inside Out-attributed numbers before publish.)*

- **Card 1:** **370+** | Law enforcement officers trained
- **Card 2:** **2,486+** | Calls answered by a clinician and officer together
- **Card 3:** **98%** | Of those calls kept out of jail

> Source basis: Heart of Iowa Community Services regional reporting. These ring true as ballpark order-of-magnitude stats for the program; Justin to confirm exact figures Inside Out is comfortable publishing on its own page (or swap to Inside Out-specific numbers).

### What is CIT?
- **H2:** What CIT actually means
- **Body:**

> "CIT" stands for Crisis Intervention Team — a partnership between Inside Out and local law enforcement that changes who shows up when someone is in a mental health crisis. Instead of an officer arriving alone to a situation they may not have the training for, a CIT response sends a two-person team: a mental health professional from Inside Out and a CIT-trained officer who has completed a 40-hour course in mental health response and de-escalation. They arrive together, in plain clothes, in an unmarked car.
>
> The reason it works is unhurried time. A standard 911 call moves fast — officers have other calls waiting and a narrow set of tools for how to end the encounter. A CIT response is different. The clinician and the officer can stay as long as it takes. There's no rush to make an arrest, no rush to transport, no rush to end the conversation. The first goal is to make sure the person in crisis is safe and heard. The next is to figure out, together, what they actually need — which is almost never jail.
>
> CIT is not a magic wand and we won't pretend it is. It runs in specific jurisdictions, not everywhere in Iowa. Sometimes safety still requires hospital transport. Sometimes a situation is more complex than any single response can solve. But when CIT is the team that arrives, the encounter tends to look and feel very different from the one most people imagine when they imagine calling 911.

### What you can expect (from a CIT response)
- **H2:** What it looks like when CIT arrives
- **Intro:** No sirens, no flashing lights, no uniformed officer at the door alone. Here's what a CIT response looks like in practice.

**Cards (4):**

1. **icon hint: car** | **A team of two, in plain clothes** | A mental health professional and a CIT-trained officer arrive together, in an unmarked vehicle and ordinary clothing — so the moment doesn't draw stares from neighbors or escalate before anyone has spoken.

2. **icon hint: clock** | **As much time as it takes** | The team isn't racing to the next call. They can stay an hour. They can stay longer. The goal is a calmer, more grounded conversation — not a fast exit.

3. **icon hint: speech bubble** | **Listening before anything else** | They introduce themselves by first name. They speak slowly. They ask what's happening and what would help. They are trained to de-escalate, not to take command.

4. **icon hint: arrow forward** | **A plan for what comes next** | Once things have steadied, the team helps figure out the next step — staying home with a safety plan, a warm hand-off to ongoing care, or transport when it's truly needed. They follow up within a week to check in.

### What we ask of you
- **H2:** How to help us help
- **Intro:** If you're the person calling for help — for yourself or someone else — a few things make a CIT response more likely and more effective.

**Cards (4):**

1. **icon hint: phone** | **Say it's a mental health crisis** | When you call 911, the very first thing to tell the dispatcher is that this is a mental health situation, not a crime. That single sentence helps the dispatcher route a CIT-trained response when one is available.

2. **icon hint: shield-check** | **Share what's safe to share** | Mention any diagnoses, medications, recent stressors, or weapons in the home. None of this is to label the person — it's to help the responders show up prepared and calm.

3. **icon hint: pause** | **Lower the temperature where you can** | Move pets, kids, or other people to another room if it's safe to do so. Turn down music. Keep your own voice low. The room a responder walks into shapes the first sixty seconds.

4. **icon hint: heart** | **Stay nearby, stay steady** | You don't have to manage the crisis. You do have to keep yourself okay enough to be present. Sit where the person can see you. Don't argue with what they're feeling. Help is coming.

### How CIT works in your community
- **H2:** How to access CIT
- **Step 1:** **H3:** Call 911 (or our crisis line if it's safer to talk first) | **Body:** If you or someone you love is in immediate danger, call 911 and tell the dispatcher this is a mental health crisis. If you're not in immediate danger and you'd rather start with a conversation, call our 24/7 crisis line at **844-428-3878** and we can help you decide what to do next.

- **Step 2:** **H3:** The dispatcher routes the call | **Body:** In jurisdictions where Inside Out's CIT operates, the 911 dispatcher can send a CIT response — a mental health professional and a CIT-trained officer together. Outside those jurisdictions, the responding officer may still be CIT-trained even without a clinician partner; the dispatcher will route the most appropriate team available.

- **Step 3:** **H3:** The team arrives — and stays | **Body:** The clinician and officer arrive in an unmarked car, in plain clothes, and take the time to talk. Once things have steadied, they work with the person and the family on what's next — and follow up within a week to make sure the plan is holding.

### Coverage
- **H2:** Where CIT runs today
- **Intro:** Inside Out's Crisis Intervention Team currently partners with police departments in the **Des Moines metro** — including **Clive, Urbandale, Waukee, and West Des Moines.** In these jurisdictions, a CIT clinician is dispatched alongside a CIT-trained officer.
- CIT-trained officers operate in additional partner agencies across the region *(placeholder per Justin's 2026-05-22 directive — full list to be confirmed)*, and we're working with our funding and training partners to expand co-responder coverage over time.
- **If your town isn't on this list:** Call **844-428-3878** anyway. We can talk you through your options — including Mobile Crisis Response, our 24-hour crisis stabilization programs, and how to communicate with your local 911 dispatcher about a mental health call. You should not be alone in figuring this out.

## Per-service template variations

This page diverges from the Crisis Stabilization template in three important ways the builder needs to know about:

1. **"Admissions" becomes "How to access CIT."** There is no traditional admission — the person doesn't come *to* Inside Out, the team goes *to* them. The 3-step section frames the path from 911 call → dispatch → on-scene response. The CTA card at the bottom of that section should still be a phone-line callout, but should soften the language from "You don't have to wait until it gets worse" to something like "If you're not sure whether to call 911, call us first" — the persona has a different decision in front of them than a stabilization-seeker does.

2. **"What you can expect" becomes "What it looks like when CIT arrives."** Same card-grid structure, different framing — the cards describe the encounter, not a multi-day program.

3. **The hero sub is longer than the other service pages' subs.** This is intentional and load-bearing. The persona needs to be told, in the hero, that the response is different from what they fear — otherwise they bounce. Recommend the build allow the sub to wrap to 3 lines on desktop, 5–6 on mobile, without truncation.

4. **Provider callout bar (section 5) needs an audience flip.** On stabilization pages the callout reads "Referring a client?" — addressed to clinicians. On the CIT page it should read **"Are you a law enforcement agency or county partner?"** with copy directing to the Partner Providers hub for CIT training, MOU information, and program partnership. *Flag this for the implementer.*

## Generalization check

Pass-through of every section against "would this still ring true for someone whose specific situation differs from the persona?":

- **Hero H1/sub** — No mention of bipolar, parent, son, age, or diagnosis. "Someone you love" is broad enough to include self, family, neighbor, or community member. The phrase "when someone is in crisis" works for the person in crisis themselves reading the page. ✅
- **What is CIT body** — Talks only about "someone in a mental health crisis" and "the person in crisis." No demographics. ✅
- **What it looks like (cards)** — No persona-specific framing. The "team of two, in plain clothes" card explicitly mentions neighbors and stigma, which broadens the audience (not narrows it). ✅
- **How to help us help (cards)** — Reads for both self-callers and third-party callers. Card 4 ("stay nearby, stay steady") assumes a third-party caller — flagging as a near-miss. **Mitigation:** Card 4's intro phrase "If you're the person calling for help — for yourself or someone else" already explicitly covers both. Acceptable, but the implementer should ensure the section intro stays visible above the cards.
- **How CIT works** — Step 1 explicitly addresses "you or someone you love." Steps 2 and 3 are situation-neutral. ✅
- **Coverage** — Names jurisdictions, not populations. ✅

Net: no bipolar-specific, parent-specific, age-specific, or diagnosis-specific copy remains. The persona's anxiety is honored in the *tone* of the hero and the "what to expect" cards without being named in the *content*.

## Partner-provider perspective

The CIT page has the strongest dual-audience tension of any Inside Out service. Law enforcement agencies, ER discharge planners, prosecutors, county sheriffs, and mental health court coordinators all have reasons to be on this page — and they read it very differently from the persona.

**What partner providers need to see:**

- **That Inside Out is a credible, professional partner.** Specifically: that Inside Out clinicians are licensed, that the program is structured around the Memphis-model 40-hour training standard, that there is a defined MOU framework with partner agencies, and that funding flow is transparent (HICS funds officer training participation).
- **Concrete operational language.** What hours does the co-responder team operate? What's the dispatch protocol? What's the hand-off model when transport is needed (Mental Health Access Centers, ER, crisis stabilization, jail diversion intake)? What's the follow-up loop?
- **Outcomes data.** Jail diversion counts. Repeat-contact reduction. Use-of-force incident comparisons. Hospital transport rates. Whatever Inside Out is comfortable disclosing.
- **A path to engage.** "How does our agency become a partner?" — this is the key question. Likely funnels to the Partner Providers hub, specifically to a CIT-partnership-inquiry sub-page or contact.

**How to handle this on the page without breaking the consumer voice:**

The body copy stays empathetic and community-facing. The handoff to provider-language happens through:

1. **The provider callout bar (section 5)**, modified to read something like:
   > **Are you a law enforcement agency, ER, or county partner?** Inside Out's CIT team works with police departments, sheriff's offices, and partner providers across the Heart of Iowa region. Learn how to partner with us, access training resources, and view co-response outcomes.
   > *CTA: Partner Hub → CIT for Agencies*

2. **A dedicated "For Partner Agencies" anchor section** (optional — could be added if Justin wants it on the service page itself rather than only in the Provider Hub). One paragraph + 3 link tiles: (a) CIT training & 40-hour course info, (b) Partnership inquiry / MOU template, (c) Outcomes & reporting.

**Language that earns law-enforcement trust:**

- "Memphis-model 40-hour training" — recognized national standard, says we're not improvising.
- "Co-responder model" or "alongside" — clarifies the clinician is partnering, not replacing.
- "HICS-funded officer participation" — signals there's a financial structure already in place.
- Reference to the Sequential Intercept Model (used by Polk County and Stepping Up nationally) if Justin's program aligns with it — strong signal to county officials.
- Avoid language that reads as critical of police on the consumer side of the page if a sheriff is going to scroll past it. The community-facing copy should not say "police might hurt you" — it should say what CIT *adds*. Re-read drafts with a partnering chief in mind; the current draft passes this test (we name the fear in tone, not in finger-pointing).

**Language to avoid for partner providers:**

- Anything that frames CIT as "instead of police" (it's not — it's *with* police).
- Activist language like "alternative to policing." This is not Denver STAR; this is co-responder.
- Promising 24/7 coverage if we don't have it. Be specific about hours.

## Open questions for Justin

1. **Stats — what can we publish?** Are the HICS regional figures (370+ officers trained, 2,486+ calls, 98% jail diversion) appropriate to cite on the Inside Out page directly, or should we frame them as "Heart of Iowa region" outcomes? Do we have any Inside Out-specific numbers to use instead?
2. **Hours of co-responder coverage.** Is the clinician-paired CIT response available 24/7? Business hours? Evenings? This is a specific question the persona will ask, and the page is weaker if we don't answer it. (Default assumption in the draft: not specified — please confirm.)
3. **Jurisdictions confirmed.** The draft names Clive, Urbandale, Waukee, West Des Moines (from your existing site). Should we add Dallas County, Adel, Guthrie County, Woodward, Perry as "CIT-trained officer" jurisdictions even though the clinician partner may not ride along there? How honestly do we want to draw this line?
4. **Dispatch protocol — what should we tell callers to say?** Does a caller need to ask for CIT by name, or does the dispatcher route automatically based on call type? This shapes the "What we ask of you" card #1 copy. (Default assumption in the draft: just say "this is a mental health crisis," dispatcher routes.)
5. **Relationship to Mobile Crisis Response.** When does each apply? Working assumption: CIT activates via 911 when there's a public safety dimension; Mobile Crisis activates via the Inside Out crisis line when there's no immediate safety concern. Please confirm so we can land both pages with a clean cross-link.
6. **What if my county doesn't have CIT?** The draft directs unsupported-jurisdiction callers to call Inside Out anyway. Is that the right move? Should we proactively name Foundation 2 (Linn), Broadlawns MCRT (Polk), or other partners as alternates?
7. **Should partner agencies be named on the page?** The existing Inside Out CIT page names Heart of Iowa Community Services as funder. Should we name partnering police departments by name (Clive, Urbandale, etc., per agency), or keep it general? Strong opinion: name them — it's a credibility signal and lets a Waukee resident immediately see "yes, mine."
8. **Provider callout bar copy.** Confirm the audience flip from "Referring a client?" to "Are you a law enforcement agency?" is the right move for this page.
9. **Inside Out's CIT staff.** Existing site lists Timothy Wood (CIT Manager) and Margaret Lano. Should they appear on the new page (warmer, more human), or kept off (consistent with other service pages)?

## Sources

- **Inside Out CIT page (current).** https://www.insideoutiowa.com/services/crisis-intervention-training-program — Inside Out's own existing CIT description. Authoritative for current jurisdictions, the 6-step process, partner attribution (HICS), staff names (Timothy Wood, Margaret Lano), and the FAQ on plain clothes / unmarked car. The draft is grounded in this content with tone re-worked.
- **Heart of Iowa Community Services — CIT Co-Responder Program.** https://hicsiowa.org/services/cit-co-responder-program/ — HICS is Inside Out's funding and training partner. Source for full agency partner list and program structure.
- **Trilix case study on HICS / Iowa CIT.** https://www.trilix.com/blog/helping-heart-of-iowa-community-services-support-iowans-and-law-enforcement-agencies-with-mental-hea/ — Public summary of HICS CIT model and operational details.
- **Cedar Rapids Police CIT.** https://www.cedar-rapids.org/local_government/departments_g_-_v/police/crisis_intervention_team.php — Counter-example: how *not* to communicate CIT to a frightened family member. Org-chart-style page.
- **Foundation 2 — Law Enforcement Liaisons (Linn County).** https://foundation2.org/law-enforcement-liaisons/ — Iowa co-responder model with clearer family-facing communication than most. Useful structural comparison.
- **Linn County Mental Health Access Center.** https://www.linncountyiowa.gov/1423/Mental-Health-Access-Center — Hand-off destination model worth referencing structurally.
- **Denver STAR — WellPower.** https://www.wellpower.org/star-program/ — National benchmark for trust-building public communication. Source of the "no sirens, no flashing lights, no badge" framing the hero echoes.
- **Denver STAR fact sheet.** https://www.denvergov.org/files/assets/public/v/1/public-health-and-environment/documents/cbh/star-one-pager-fact-sheet-2.pdf — Public-facing one-pager structure.
- **NAMI Iowa — What Iowa law enforcement is doing.** https://namiiowa.org/iowa-law-enforcement-better-respond-mental-illness/ — Source of "go low and slow," first-name introductions, "we came to help, not to arrest or hurt" — the trust-building phrases echoed in tone throughout.
- **NACo — Counties 'Step Up' in Polk County, Iowa.** https://www.naco.org/articles/counties-step-polk-county-iowa — Background on Stepping Up initiative, Polk County's MCRT, and the Sequential Intercept Model context relevant to the partner-provider section.
- **Polk County Sequential Intercept Model report.** https://www.polkcountyiowa.gov/media/bqlbetms/may-2023-polk-county-sim-report.pdf — Reference for SIM framing if used in partner-provider language.
- **Broadlawns Crisis Services.** https://www.broadlawns.org/clinics-and-services/mental-health/crisis-services — Polk County crisis service partner context.
- **NAMI National — CIT Programs.** https://www.nami.org/Advocacy/Crisis-Intervention/Crisis-Intervention-Team-(CIT)-Programs — National advocacy framing for the 40-hour Memphis model standard.
